title: SEGMENT 14 — Target four, folate metabolism: the sulfonamides and trimethoprim

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — Target four, folate metabolism: the sulfonamides and trimethoprim
Fourth target: folate synthesis, and this is, to my taste, the most elegant selective-toxicity story in all of antimicrobial pharmacology, so I've saved it for a moment when you can appreciate it.
Recall the setup from the beginning: both bacteria and humans need folate to build the nucleotide bases for DNA.
But you get your folate ready-made from your diet — it's a vitamin for you.
Most bacteria cannot import folate; they must synthesize it from scratch, starting from a precursor called PABA, para-aminobenzoic acid.
So bacteria run a folate factory that you simply don't possess.
Attack the factory, and you've found a target that has no equivalent in the host at all.
This is target category four, and it's the basis for one of medicine's oldest antibiotic strategies.
The sulfonamides — the "sulfa" drugs, like sulfamethoxazole — were among the very first antibacterials.
Here's the mechanism, and it's almost poetically simple: the sulfonamide molecule is a structural mimic of PABA, the natural starting material for folate synthesis.

The first enzyme in the bacterial folate pathway, dihydropteroate synthase, grabs the sulfonamide thinking it's PABA — a case of mistaken identity, just like the beta-lactam fooling the transpeptidase — and the enzyme is either blocked or it builds a useless dead-end product instead of folate.
Either way, folate production grinds down.
It's competitive inhibition by molecular impersonation.
And because your cells don't have this enzyme or this pathway at all, the selectivity is excellent.
Now, on its own, blocking that one step is only bacteriostatic — it slows folate production but bacteria can limp along on reserves.
The masterstroke is combination.
There's a second drug, trimethoprim, that blocks a later enzyme in the very same pathway — dihydrofolate reductase, which converts dihydrofolate to the active tetrahydrofolate.
So now picture the folate assembly line with two drugs hitting two sequential steps: the sulfonamide blocks an early step, trimethoprim blocks a later one.
This is called sequential blockade, and the effect is synergistic — the two drugs together are far more powerful than the sum of their separate effects, and the combination becomes bactericidal.

That combination — trimethoprim plus sulfamethoxazole, which you'll see abbreviated everywhere — is used widely, classically for urinary tract infections and for a particular pneumonia in immunocompromised patients.
And here's the selectivity capstone that makes trimethoprim so clever.
You might object: wait, humans also have a dihydrofolate reductase enzyme — don't we risk blocking our own?
Yes, we do have that enzyme — but trimethoprim binds the bacterial version of the enzyme tens of thousands of times more tightly than it binds the human version.
The bacterial and human enzymes are different enough in shape that the drug discriminates between them.
So at therapeutic doses, trimethoprim shuts down the bacterial enzyme while barely touching yours.

That selective affinity for the bacterial form of an enzyme that both species share — that is selective toxicity achieved not by attacking a unique target, but by exploiting subtle structural differences in a shared one.
It's the same principle as the 70S-versus-80S ribosome, expressed in a single enzyme's binding pocket.
A lovely note to close the target categories on.
